informal: Show that $x^{4} \equiv 2(p)$ has a solution for $p \equiv 1(4)$ iff $p$ is of the form $A^{2}+64 B^{2}$.
lean:
theorem Ireland_Rosen_exercise_5_28 {p : ℕ} (hp : p.Prime) (hp1 : p ≡ 1 [MOD 4]) :
    (∃ x, x ^ 4 ≡ 2 [MOD p]) ↔ ∃ A B, p = A ^ 2 + 64 * B ^ 2
reasoning: The informal statement asserts that for a prime p ≡ 1 (mod 4), x^4 ≡ 2 (mod p) is solvable iff p = A² + 64B². The Lean signature encodes exactly this biconditional, with hypotheses p prime and p ≡ 1 (mod 4). The existential ∃ x with x : ℕ is equivalent to solvability over ℤ/p (any residue class has a natural representative). The form A² + 64B² with A, B : ℕ is equivalent to the integer version since squares only depend on absolute values, so allowing A, B ∈ ℤ gives the same set of representable p. Quantifier structure and conclusion match the informal claim.
